lens.included_activities: Independent representatives that sell media time or space for media owners and provide repeat account management, prospecting, proposal, contract, and campaign-support work to external media-owner customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Media-owner captive sales teams, media buying agencies, advertising agencies, software-only ad-placement products, non-control investments, and representatives whose work is solely a one-off transaction without a repeat outsourced-service relationship.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The customer is a media owner; the representative is commonly compensated through commissions or other performance-linked revenue tied to selling the owner's inventory, with recurring account relationships and campaign renewals where present.

a.bridge: S1 measures tasks for advertising sales agents across employing industries, not wage-weighted work inside NAICS 541840. I mapped its documented proposals, account paperwork, cost estimates, research, and sales-material work to AI-assisted drafting, research, CRM, and administrative automation, while retaining relationship building, persuasion, negotiation, and accountable advice as less substitutable. This likely overstates exposure if independent representatives spend more time in relationship-led selling, and understates it if their back office is unusually manual.
a.rationale: The occupation contains substantial information, proposal, documentation, cost-estimation, and account-processing work, but also continuous persuasion, negotiation, client rapport, and judgment. The range reflects an unmeasured NAICS-specific occupation and wage mix.
a.change_evidence: A representative sample time-and-motion study of independent media representatives, or payroll-by-SOC data for NAICS 541840 combined with task diaries, would permit wage weighting and materially revise the estimate.
caveats: The occupation includes employees outside the target NAICS and omits proprietor labor.
No observed wage-weighted task allocation is available for the frozen lens.

rho.rationale: Drafting, research, CRM updates, and administrative work can be operationalized with review controls, but proposals, pricing, client-specific claims, and contract commitments need human approval. The relationship-led portion also limits workflow removal within the horizon.
rho.change_evidence: Implementation evidence from independent media-representative firms, including before-and-after staffing, CRM adoption logs, error rates, and customer-retention cohorts, would materially move this estimate.
caveats: This is a judgment about realized operational adoption rather than a measured adoption rate.
AI policy, client approval requirements, and the inventory systems of media owners can vary materially by firm.

e.rationale: The Census definition is independent representation for media owners, which normally is an external service and can recur across inventory sales and account renewals. The discount allows for establishments with episodic representation, insufficient operating depth, or revenue arrangements that do not meet the frozen lens.
e.change_evidence: A NAICS 541840 firm survey or a commercial company database review that identifies client concentration, contract renewal, ownership, and normalized EBITDA would directly test lens eligibility.
caveats: The classification definition identifies activity, not recurring revenue, size-adjusted profitability, or control eligibility.
The supplied firm-count input is not re-estimated here.

s5.rationale: A small specialist representation firm can be transferable when accounts, media-owner agreements, sales processes, and management depth survive the owner, but personal relationships and commission-dependent revenue make many firms less transferable. No observed NAICS-specific control-transfer rate was located.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-deal data and lost-deal reasons from named lower-middle-market brokers, plus owner-age and succession responses in a NAICS 541840 survey, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: This is not an observed sale probability.
Owner dependence, agreement assignability, and customer concentration can create wider variation than the aggregate range conveys.

q.bridge: S2 reports that advertising-sales-agent compensation commonly includes commissions and bonuses linked to sales, but it does not measure contractual benefit retention for independent media representatives. I use performance-linked compensation and monthly sales pressure as evidence that efficiency gains face commission, customer, and competitive pass-through; the resulting retained-benefit range is a judgment. The proxy likely understates firm-level retention where contracts have fixed commissions and overstates it where sellers or media owners capture the gain.
q.rationale: Commission-linked selling and competitive quotas reduce confidence that a representative can retain all labor savings. Recurring account knowledge and service quality can preserve part of the benefit, particularly when savings improve coverage rather than reduce customer-facing effort.
q.change_evidence: Executed representative agreements, commission schedules, renewal-price histories, and customer-switching data from a sample of target firms would directly measure retention.
caveats: BLS evidence concerns workers across the occupation rather than independent firms under the lens.
Contracts may be fixed-fee, commission, salary-plus-commission, or negotiated individually.

d5.bridge: S2 projects total US advertising-sales-agent employment, not constant-price, constant-quality demand for NAICS 541840 services. I translate the documented occupation decline and its stated drivers into a five-year demand proxy, allowing a wider range because employment also reflects productivity and the NAICS excludes some employer types. The occupation projection may overstate contraction of durable outsourced demand if AI raises productivity without reducing service quantity, and may understate it if self-service placement displaces representatives faster than projected.
d5.rationale: BLS expects fewer advertising sales agents as print circulation falls and digital placement automates, while also stating that digital advertising continues to grow and agents remain needed. That supports broadly flat to modestly declining demand rather than a single observed NAICS demand forecast.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted NAICS 541840 receipts, media-owner outsourcing spend, and renewal-volume cohorts by channel would directly measure the current service basket and materially update this proxy.
caveats: Employment is not equivalent to constant-price service quantity.
The BLS projection spans a longer interval than the frozen horizon and covers a broader occupation.

o.bridge: S2 states that digital ad placement can be automated but that agents are still needed in digital media, and S1 documents relationship, persuasion, negotiation, and client-advice tasks. Neither source measures the future share of NAICS 541840 service quantity requiring an accountable operator. I infer that automation removes a meaningful share of transactional placement but leaves operator work for relationship management, selling, and complex inventory; this may overstate durability if self-serve platforms displace account work and understate it for specialized media inventory.
o.rationale: Automated placement directly challenges the transactional core of the service, but the source evidence indicates continued need for agents and documents customer-facing, negotiated, and advisory work that software alone may not supply.
o.change_evidence: Channel-level media-owner data comparing self-serve inventory, managed-service revenue, and representative-managed renewal cohorts would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The result depends heavily on media channel, inventory complexity, and whether the media owner preserves third-party representation.
No source directly measures operator-required quantity for the exact lens.

narrative.executive_view: Media Representatives consists of independent firms selling media time or space for media owners. The work has real administrative and proposal-generation exposure, but its transferability and demand are constrained by relationship dependence and automated digital placement.
narrative.ai_impact: Advertising sales work includes prospect research, account paperwork, proposal and media-kit preparation, cost estimates, reporting, and contract drafting. Those workflows can be accelerated with controlled AI, whereas persuasion, customer discovery, negotiation, and relationship maintenance remain operator-intensive.
narrative.value_capture: Sales compensation often includes commissions and bonuses, and sales quotas create competitive pressure. An operator may retain some productivity benefit through better account coverage and lower back-office load, but commission terms, customer pricing, and competitive service levels can pass a substantial portion through.
narrative.firm_availability: The classification specifically covers independent representatives, which fits external outsourced service. Whether a particular lower-middle-market firm is transferable depends on assignable media-owner agreements, recurring accounts, management depth, and how much revenue remains tied to the seller-owner.
narrative.demand_durability: BLS expects advertising-sales employment to decline as print weakens and digital placement automates, even while digital advertising grows and still uses agents. The durable portion is therefore likely concentrated in managed relationships and complex inventory rather than routine placement.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: No direct public measure was found for NAICS-specific transfer probability, benefit retention, operator-required quantity, or wage-weighted task exposure. The occupation evidence covers a broader workforce, and firm outcomes can vary sharply with channel mix, customer concentration, contract assignability, and self-service substitution.
narrative.principal_driver: AI can reduce the proposal, research, documentation, and account-administration burden while preserving customer-facing sales coverage.
narrative.principal_weakness: Self-service digital placement and owner-dependent relationships can erode both service demand and transferability.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Advertising Sales Agents
sources.supports: Advertising-sales-agent tasks, work activities, and client-contact requirements used as an occupation proxy.

sources.title: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook: Advertising Sales Agents
sources.supports: Documented duties, performance-linked compensation, employment outlook, and digital-placement automation context.

sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau NAICS 2022: Media Representatives
sources.supports: Official industry definition of independent representatives selling media time or space for media owners.
